source.title: Bio-mechanisms of Swimming and Flying: Fluid Dynamics, Biomimetic Robots, and Sports Science
source.note: Reference values extracted/paraphrased for use as a comparison benchmark in a video-based motion analysis program. Original chapters remain copyrighted by Springer and the respective authors.

swimming_human_model_SWUM.title: Analysis of Breast, Back and Butterfly Strokes by the Swimming Human Simulation Model SWUM
swimming_human_model_SWUM.author: Motomu Nakashima

video_derivation_method.method: Joint angles manually digitized per frame by matching body posture in source video to the simulation model.
video_derivation_method.source_videos: Official Japan Swimming Federation educational 'model swimming' videos (non-competitive intensity).

joint_degrees_of_freedom.crawl: not specified in this chapter (baseline of prior referenced study)

variables.U: swimming speed averaged over one stroke cycle
variables.T: drag force on the swimmer's body towed at speed U in gliding position
variables.P: average mechanical power consumed by the swimmer

normalized_stroke_length_vs_actual_video.breaststroke: simulation underestimates actual value by approximately 35%

qualitative_notes: Breaststroke: hand-generated thrust peak occurs before the larger leg-kick thrust peak.
Backstroke/Butterfly: peak hand 'push' thrust closely precedes peak body velocity.
Most strokes show a negative (decelerating) thrust phase during the arm's recovery motion - a possible technique-efficiency indicator from video.

Reference comparison: front-crawl propelling efficiency reported elsewhere in literature (Toussaint et al. 1988) as 0.46-0.77, notably higher than this model's estimate - flagged by the original author as an open discrepancy.

hand_palm_fluid_dynamics.title: Research in Fluid Dynamical Specification of Hand Palms in Freestyle Swimming
hand_palm_fluid_dynamics.author: Shinichiro Ito

sweepback_angle_gamma_definition.45_deg: arm moves outward from the body's long axis
sweepback_angle_gamma_definition.90_deg: arm moves parallel to the body's long axis
sweepback_angle_gamma_definition.135_deg: arm moves inward toward the body's long axis
sweepback_angle_gamma_definition.usage: Approximates arm-path phase during an S-shaped pull stroke (catch / pull-sculling / finish).

test_conditions.reynolds_number: ~3.4e5, matched to competitive freestyle underwater swimming speed

hand_shapes_tested.Hand_A: fingers spread, ~3mm gaps - common competitive swimmer configuration
hand_shapes_tested.Delta_wing_reference: flat plate, 39.5 degree vertex angle, used as simplified analog of a closed hand

key_findings: Lift/drag generation mechanism of a hand palm closely resembles that of a delta wing (leading-edge vortex separation, not 2D airfoil theory).
Closed-finger hand (Hand C) polar curve closely matches delta wing polar curve, including a discontinuity near angle of attack ~45-50 degrees.
Spread-finger hand (Hand A) lacks this discontinuity, attributed to flow leakage through the finger gaps from palm side to back side.
Vortex strength is stronger on the little-finger side than the thumb side, due to flow leakage through the thumb/index-finger gap.
Prior literature (Ito & Okuno 2003) found I-shaped (direct drag) pull stroke produces greater thrust than the traditional S-shaped pull stroke.

supplementary_context.topic: Passive vs. powered posture/trajectory control in swimming fish
supplementary_context.useful_for: Classifying observed body motion in video as passive (automatic, structure-driven) vs. powered (actively controlled fin/limb movement).
A measurable phase delay exists between the leading edge and mid-section of an oscillating caudal fin during undulation - relevant if analyzing tail/fin video kinematics.

supplementary_context.topic: CNS control of fish undulatory swimming (review)
supplementary_context.useful_for: Theoretical background only (central pattern generators); no extractable numeric benchmarks.

supplementary_context.topic: PIV flow measurement around a freely swimming fish
supplementary_context.useful_for: Confirms a reverse Karman vortex street forms behind the tail fin during propulsion - relevant for flow-visualization-based analysis.

suggested_program_integration: Compare video-tracked joint angle count/range per stroke against the DOF benchmarks (34/28/35) to identify under-tracked joints.
If swim speed, estimated drag, and power output are available, compute eta = U*T/P and benchmark against 0.20 (crawl/back/butterfly) or 0.036 (breaststroke).
Use normalized stroke length (distance per cycle / swimmer height) as a fast comparative metric across swimmers without needing full power/drag estimates.
Flag a 'recovery-phase deceleration' segment in tracked center-of-mass velocity as a possible technique-efficiency indicator.
Classify tracked arm-sweep angle into ~45/90/135 degree bins to auto-detect catch/pull/finish stroke phases from video.
